title: Describe Symptoms Clearly
summary: Describe how you feel to a medical professional using clear, specific language.
learnerPromise: You will be able to explain your symptoms to a doctor, nurse, or pharmacist in a way that helps them understand what is going on.
whyItMatters: Clear descriptions help medical professionals assess your situation more accurately and suggest the right next steps.
realLifeUses: Telling a doctor about a stomach problem during a clinic visit
Describing pain to a nurse over the phone when booking an appointment
Answering a pharmacist's questions about your symptoms to get the right medicine
Reporting changes in how you feel at a follow-up appointment
materialsNeeded: Pen or digital device for writing
result: A written symptom note you could read or hand to a medical professional.
youWillLearn: Use specific words to describe pain, discomfort, or changes in your body
Track when symptoms started and what makes them better or worse
Organize your description so it is easy for a professional to follow
Mention relevant details like recent activities, foods, or medicines
miniChallenge: Think of a time you felt unwell. Write down a clear description using the what, when, where, and how much framework.
steps: Write down what the symptom feels like using specific words like sharp, dull, burning, or throbbing.
Note when it started and whether it is constant or comes and goes.
Describe where on your body you feel it and if it spreads to other areas.
Rate how strong it is on a scale of 1 to 10, with 10 being the worst.
List anything that makes it better, worse, or anything you have already tried.
completionCriteria: You have a written note that covers what, when, where, and how strong the symptom is
You can read your note aloud in under one minute
A listener could understand the basic situation without extra explanation
commonProblems: Forgetting details when you are nervous at the appointment
Using vague words like weird or funny that do not give useful information
Trying to diagnose yourself instead of just describing what you feel
tips: Write your notes before the appointment and bring them with you
Use comparisons like tight as a rubber band or sharp like a needle if that helps
Be honest about things you think might be embarrassing; medical professionals have heard it before
helpBoundary: If your symptoms are severe, getting worse quickly, or you feel unsafe, contact emergency services or an urgent care center rather than waiting to describe them in a scheduled appointment.
